subject: Psychology
grade_subject_fit: Source-derived Psychology 11/12 Cycle A A1 package based on the original teacher binder, student packet, and A1 slide deck structure.
unit_context: Cycle A A1, Psychology as a Science. Source pattern: teacher 10-day sequence, student vocabulary organizer, handouts, final assessment, exit tickets, and 15-slide A1 deck.
assignment_purpose: Students learn to distinguish psychology from opinion, advice, stereotypes, and unsupported claims by using vocabulary, research methods, evidence, ethics, and limits.
final_evidence_target: Flawed Claim Detective: students evaluate a psychology-style claim by identifying evidence needed, method fit, limits, alternatives, and ethical concerns.

student_task_flow.label: Vocabulary Organizer
student_task_flow.evidence: Students define A1 research terms and create examples.

student_task_flow.label: Handouts 1-6
student_task_flow.evidence: Students sort claims, operationalize variables, compare methods, and read a source for evidence and limits.

student_task_flow.label: Handouts 7-9
student_task_flow.evidence: Students practise claim detection, design a study, and sort model responses.

student_task_flow.label: Flawed Claim Detective
student_task_flow.evidence: Students evaluate a claim with method, evidence, limit, and ethics.

success_criteria: I can explain why psychology uses evidence instead of relying only on opinion, advice, or personal experience.
I can turn a vague idea into a researchable question with variables and operational definitions.
I can compare basic research methods and explain what each can and cannot show.
I can explain why correlation does not prove causation.
I can include ethical caution and limits in a psychology explanation.
supports_scaffolds: Supported, proficient, and extending final-assessment paths
differentiation_model: Supported path uses claim cards and paragraph frames. Proficient path analyzes a flawed claim independently. Extending path compares two methods and explains which gives stronger evidence and why.
checkpoint_release_logic: Students complete vocabulary and method practice before the final assessment. Teacher checks for one operational definition, one method limitation, one correlation limit, and one ethical concern before releasing the final task.
teacher_implementation_notes: Source basis: Cycle A Teacher Binder, A1 Psychology as a Science, plus the Cycle A student packet A1 pathway and A1 slide deck.
Protect the unit pattern: claim, evidence, method, limits. This is the recurring move students use across Cycle A.
Use fictional, public, or general examples. Do not collect personal data from classmates without clear approval.
When students offer advice, opinions, stereotypes, or personality quiz claims, redirect to evidence and method.
The final assessment is Flawed Claim Detective. Students identify evidence needed, select or critique a method, explain limits, and address ethics.
When shortening the unit, protect the core concept, one application task, the source task, and the final assessment. Cut repeated examples first.
During assessment prep, students sort emerging, proficient, and extending responses by vocabulary accuracy, evidence use, limits, and ethical caution.
likely_misconceptions: Psychology is just opinion or advice.
A confident claim is the same as evidence.
A correlation proves one thing caused another.
A sample automatically represents a population.
A single example is enough to explain a person.
Ethics are extra rather than part of research design.
pacing_shape: Ten-day source-derived A1 unit: introduction, variables, methods, correlation, ethics, reading, claim practice, research design, assessment prep, final assessment.

teacher_guide.title: Cycle A A1 Teacher Guide — Psychology as a Science
teacher_guide.overview: Source-derived teacher guide based on the Cycle A Teacher Binder A1 section. The unit teaches students how psychologists study behaviour responsibly by moving from claim to evidence to method to limits.
teacher_guide.essential_question: How do psychologists study behaviour responsibly?
teacher_guide.big_idea: Psychology is stronger when claims are testable, methods fit the question, ethics are protected, and conclusions stay within the evidence.
teacher_guide.opening_frame: Tell students that A1 is about learning the rules of responsible psychological explanation. The course does not reward confident guessing; it rewards careful claims, evidence, method, and limits.
teacher_guide.frame_line: A1 recurring frame: claim → evidence → method → limits.
teacher_guide.assessment_focus: Final assessment: Flawed Claim Detective. Students evaluate a psychology-style claim and explain evidence needed, method fit, limits, alternative explanations, and ethical concerns.
learning_goals: Students distinguish psychology from opinion, advice, therapy, personality quizzes, stereotypes, and unsupported claims.
Students write researchable questions and operational definitions.
Students compare survey, case study, naturalistic observation, and experiment.
Students explain why correlation is not causation.
Students apply ethics and limits to claim evaluation.
materials: Cycle A Student Packet Bundle A1 pages
OpenStax Psychology 2e Chapter 1.1 and Chapter 2 research methods sections
integrity_checks: Do not collect personal data from classmates without explicit teacher approval.
Do not diagnose or label people from course vocabulary.
Require one method limitation and one ethical caution before accepting final work.
Protect privacy, consent, and debriefing language when discussing research.
teacher_notes: Use the student packet for photocopying and student-facing work. Use this guide for pacing, assessment calibration, safety language, and teacher decisions.
A strong answer uses vocabulary, evidence, an appropriate method, a limit, and ethical caution.
For support, provide claim/evidence/method/limit sentence frames.
For extension, ask students to compare two possible methods and justify which provides stronger evidence.
A1 source tracker: OpenStax Psychology 2e Chapter 2 for research methods, correlation, experiments, and ethics.
questions: What makes a psychology claim testable?
What is the difference between a vague idea and an operational definition?
Which variable changes, and which outcome is measured?
Which method best fits this research question and why?
Why does correlation not prove causation?
What ethical protection is needed before collecting evidence from people?
What does this source show, and what does it not show?
How can a claim be rewritten so it is more careful?
What careful conclusion can you actually support?
look_fors: Student defines psychology as evidence-based study rather than opinion or advice.
Student turns vague concepts into measurable variables.
Student selects a method and names what it can and cannot show.
Student explains correlation without making a causal claim.
Student names consent, privacy, potential harm, or debriefing as ethical concerns.
Student uses a source without overstating what it proves.
Student final response includes vocabulary, evidence, method, limit, and ethical caution.

timing.activity: Is this psychology? Students distinguish psychology from opinion, advice, therapy, personality quizzes, stereotypes, and research-based claims. Exit: What makes a psychology claim testable?
Questions, variables, and operational definitions. Students turn vague ideas into researchable questions and measurable variables. Exit: Write one operational definition.
Research methods. Students compare survey, case study, naturalistic observation, and experiment. Exit: Which method fits your question and why?
Correlation is not causation. Students explain why a relationship between variables does not prove cause. Exit: Why does correlation not prove causation?
Ethics in psychological research. Students identify ethical concerns and propose safer research choices. Exit: Name one ethical protection.
Reading like a psychology student. Students track definition, evidence, method, and limits from a source excerpt. Exit: What did the reading prove or not prove?
Flawed claim practice. Students identify overconfident claims and rewrite them carefully. Exit: Rewrite one overconfident claim.
Research design challenge. Students draft question, hypothesis, variables, method, sample, limit, and ethical protections. Exit: Name one design limit.
Assessment prep. Students sort emerging, proficient, and extending model responses. Exit: What makes a response proficient?
Final assessment: Flawed Claim Detective. Students evaluate a claim and write a careful conclusion. Exit: What is your careful conclusion?

matching_bank: psychology: scientific study of behaviour and mental processes
variable: something that can change in a study
operational definition: how a concept is measured
correlation: relationship between variables, not proof of cause
experiment: method that manipulates one variable
ethics: protections for people in research

model.title: Flawed Claim Detective model
steps: Claim: Students who use phones more have lower marks.
Evidence needed: clear data about phone use, marks, sample, timing, and other factors.
Possible method: correlation could show a relationship; experiment would be difficult and must be ethical.
Alternative explanations: sleep, study time, course load, stress, prior achievement, or distraction patterns.
Limit: a relationship alone does not prove phone use caused lower marks.
Ethics: protect privacy, avoid shaming, collect only needed data, and explain the purpose clearly.

content.subtitle: The science of mind and behaviour — not mind-reading, guessing, or labelling people.
bullets: claim → evidence → method → limits
mind
behaviour
evidence
limits

content.scenario: Psychology is not reading people, diagnosing classmates, personality-quiz entertainment, or I-just-know explanations.
prompts: What do people sometimes think psychology means?
What would make a claim stronger?

content.left: Mind: a student feels their heart race and thinks everyone will notice if they make a mistake.
content.right: Behaviour: the student avoids eye contact, asks to go last, or skips presentation day.
prompts: Which side can be observed directly?
What evidence would help explain the behaviour?

content.model: A stronger explanation moves in this order: claim, evidence, method, limits.
supports: Limits: what else could explain it?

bullets: Hypothesis: a testable prediction.
Evidence: information gathered through observation, measurement, or research.
Theory: a broad explanation supported over time.
Limits: what the evidence cannot show yet.
Multiple causes: behaviour usually has more than one influence.

rows: Revise the explanation as evidence improves.

slides.title: Many causes, not one simple label

content.scenario: A behaviour can have several influences at once: emotion, attention, memory, sleep, motivation, family, friends, school, culture, past learning, habits, and recent events.
prompts: Why is one simple label usually too weak?
Which influences might be inside the person?
Which influences might come from context?

bullets: Case evidence: a student skips two presentation days and looks tense when the topic comes up.
Possible explanation: anxiety, but we cannot diagnose from this.
Possible explanation: low preparation or incomplete work.
Possible explanation: past embarrassment or outside stress.
A careful answer keeps possibilities open until evidence is stronger.
No diagnosis from course vocabulary.
No identity labels from one behaviour.
Ask what evidence exists and what context matters.
Keep more than one possible cause on the table.

content.left: Weak: They avoid presentations because they are lazy.
content.right: Stronger: They may be avoiding because of anxiety, low preparation, past embarrassment, or another factor. We need more evidence.
prompts: Which version uses evidence more carefully?

bullets: OpenStax Ch. 1.1: psychology is the scientific study of mind and behaviour.
OpenStax Ch. 2.1–2.2: empirical methods, hypotheses, and testing claims.
Reading move: definition → example → evidence → limit.

prompts: How can psychology reduce snap judgment?
How can better questions improve explanations?
How can psychology help us explain behaviour without reducing people to labels?
Psychology is the scientific study of ______ and ______.
Name one mind example and one behaviour example.
Turn 'They are lazy' into a stronger explanation.

rows: Psychology studies mind and behaviour scientifically.
Evidence is stronger than confident guessing.
A hypothesis is testable; a theory is broad and supported over time.
One behaviour does not equal a diagnosis or identity.
Good explanations include context, multiple causes, and limits.

task_sheet.title: Cycle A A1 Student Packet — Psychology as a Science
task_sheet.purpose_line: Source-derived A1 packet pathway: vocabulary, method practice, source reading, claim detective practice, research design, assessment prep, final assessment, and exit tickets.
instructions: Use fictional, public, or general examples when topics feel personal.
Include a limit or alternative explanation in every final answer.
Do not diagnose or label people.

tasks.label: Vocabulary Organizer
tasks.prompt: Define and give an example for: psychology, hypothesis, variable, operational definition, sample, population, survey, case study, naturalistic observation, experiment, independent variable, dependent variable, confounding variable, correlation, causation, ethics.

tasks.label: Handout 1 — Is This Psychology?
tasks.prompt: Sort claims into opinion, advice, therapy, stereotype, personality quiz, or research-based claim. Explain what evidence would make each claim stronger.

tasks.label: Handout 2 — Make It Measurable
tasks.prompt: Choose a vague idea such as stress, confidence, focus, memory, or motivation. Name variables and write operational definitions.

tasks.label: Handout 3 — Research Method Match
tasks.prompt: Match research questions to survey, case study, naturalistic observation, or experiment. Explain why the method fits and name one limitation.

tasks.label: Handout 4 — The Causation Trap
tasks.prompt: For each correlation, write two alternative explanations. Identify direction problems and third variables. Use: This shows a relationship, but it does not prove...

tasks.label: Handout 5 — Ethics Check
tasks.prompt: For each study idea, identify one ethical concern. Name consent, privacy, potential harm, or debriefing needs and explain how the design could be safer.

tasks.label: Handout 6 — Reading Like a Psychology Student
tasks.prompt: Use a teacher-selected source excerpt. Track definition, example, evidence, method, and limit. Answer: what does the evidence show and not show?

tasks.label: Handout 7 — Claim Detective Practice
tasks.prompt: Choose a weak psychology-style claim. Identify the claim, evidence needed, best method, alternative explanation, ethics, and careful rewrite.

tasks.label: Handout 8 — Research Design Challenge
tasks.prompt: Write a research question, hypothesis, variables, method, sample, limit, and ethical protections. Peer-check for clarity, measurability, and caution.

tasks.label: Handout 9 — Assessment Prep: Model Response Sort
tasks.prompt: Compare emerging, proficient, and extending responses. Highlight concept use, evidence, limits, and ethics. Plan your own final response.

tasks.label: Final Assessment — Flawed Claim Detective
tasks.prompt: Evaluate a psychology-style claim. Explain evidence needed, what method could study it, what the evidence would and would not prove, and what ethical concerns matter.

tasks.label: Exit Ticket Set
tasks.prompt: Answer the ten A1 exit prompts: testable claim, operational definition, method fit, correlation limit, ethical protection, reading limit, careful rewrite, design limit, proficient response, careful conclusion.

embedded_supports: Frame: This claim would need evidence showing...
Frame: A good operational definition would measure...
Frame: This method can show..., but it cannot show...
Frame: This relationship does not prove causation because...
Frame: One ethical protection would be...
success_criteria: I used evidence from the case, source, or scenario.
I explained why the method fits.
I included a limit, alternative explanation, or caution.
I avoided diagnosis, ranking, or overclaiming.

exit_ticket.title: A1 Exit Ticket Bank
exit_ticket.prompt: Choose the current A1 exit prompt and answer in 2-4 sentences: testable claim, operational definition, method fit, correlation limit, ethical protection, reading limit, careful rewrite, design limit, proficient response, or careful conclusion.
